steps.title: Find the GCF of all three terms
steps.content:
The polynomial is \(3x^2 + 57x + 15\). Check divisibility of each coefficient by small integers:
\(3 = 3 \cdot 1\), \(57 = 3 \cdot 19\), \(15 = 3 \cdot 5\).
The greatest common factor of 3, 57, and 15 is \(3\).

steps.title: Factor out the GCF
steps.content:
Write the polynomial with 3 factored out:
$$3x^2 + 57x + 15 = 3(x^2 + 19x + 5)$$
The expression \(x^2 + 19x + 5\) has no rational roots (discriminant \(= 361 - 20 = 341\), not a perfect square), so it cannot factor further over the integers.

steps.title: Evaluate all choices
steps.content:
• A: \(3x^2\) — this is not a factor of the polynomial; dividing \(3x^2 + 57x + 15\) by \(3x^2\) does not yield a polynomial. Eliminated.
  • B: \(5x\) — \(5x\) does not divide \(3x^2\) evenly (3 is not divisible by 5). Eliminated.
  • C: \(3\) — divides every term: \(3x^2 \div 3 = x^2\), \(57x \div 3 = 19x\), \(15 \div 3 = 5\). Valid factor.
  • D: \(57x\) — \(57x\) does not divide \(3x^2\) evenly. Eliminated.
That matches choice C — C is correct.
